sentiment.rationale: Amos opens with a roaring declaration of judgment upon the nations surrounding Israel for their cruelties and transgressions.
Judgment extends to Moab, Judah, and especially Israel, cataloguing their sins of oppression and ingratitude despite divine deliverance.
A prophetic warning emphasizing that God reveals His secrets to prophets before bringing judgment upon a chosen but disobedient people.
Repeatedly declares Israel did not return to the Lord despite escalating punishments—famine, drought, pestilence, and overthrow.
A lamentation over Israel with urgent calls to seek the Lord and live; condemns hollow worship and social injustice.
Pronounces woe upon the complacent in Zion who live in luxury while ignoring the affliction of God's people.
Three visions of judgment (locusts, fire, plumbline) with Amos interceding; his confrontation with Amaziah the priest.
The vision of summer fruit symbolizes Israel's end; a famine of hearing the word of the Lord is prophesied.
Opens with unavoidable judgment but concludes with the glorious restoration of the tabernacle of David and abundant blessings.

missingCharacters.name: Amaziah
missingCharacters.description: Priest of Bethel who opposed Amos and told him to flee to Judah and prophesy there instead.

summaries.summary: Amos declares God's judgment upon Damascus, Gaza, Tyre, Edom, and Ammon for their violent transgressions against neighboring peoples.
Judgment extends to Moab, Judah, and Israel; Israel is condemned for oppressing the poor, profaning holy things, and rejecting God's prophets.
God declares that He does nothing without revealing His secret to the prophets; Israel's privilege brings greater accountability and coming punishment.
Israel is rebuked for oppression and empty worship; despite repeated divine chastisements they have not returned to the Lord.
A lamentation over fallen Israel with urgent calls to seek the Lord and practice justice; God rejects their hypocritical feasts and offerings.
Woe is pronounced upon those at ease in Zion, living in self-indulgent luxury while ignoring the ruin of the nation.
Three visions of judgment (locusts, fire, plumbline) are shown to Amos; Amaziah the priest confronts Amos and is told of his own doom.
The vision of summer fruit signals Israel's end; a coming famine of hearing God's word is foretold along with national mourning.
Though no sinner can escape God's judgment, the chapter closes with the promised restoration of the tabernacle of David and agricultural abundance.

crossReferences.note: The Lord does nothing without revealing His secret to His servants the prophets — foundational LDS doctrine of continuing revelation.
Let judgment run down as waters and righteousness as a mighty stream.
A famine of hearing the words of the Lord — parallels the Book of Mormon's description of plain and precious things being taken from the Bible.
James quotes Amos at the Jerusalem Council regarding the restoration of the tabernacle of David.

verses.reason: 'Surely the Lord God will do nothing, but he revealeth his secret unto his servants the prophets' — cornerstone of LDS doctrine on continuing revelation.
'Prepare to meet thy God, O Israel' — a solemn and frequently quoted call to readiness.
'Let judgment run down as waters, and righteousness as a mighty stream' — a classic call for social justice.
Amos declares he was not a prophet's son but a herdman and gatherer of sycamore fruit — illustrating God's calling of the humble.
'A famine in the land, not a famine of bread... but of hearing the words of the Lord' — a prophecy of apostasy.
Promise to raise up the tabernacle of David — quoted by James in Acts 15 as fulfilled through the gospel going to the Gentiles.
